coredata.dc:title: Flower-like nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots, MoS2, and dendritic Ag-based Z-scheme type photocatalysts for effective tartrazine degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Teymourinia, Hakimeh
Rtimi, Sami
Ghalkhani, Masoumeh
Ramazani, Ali
Aminabhavi, Tejraj M.
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites of carbon quantum dots, molybdenum disulfide, and dendritic silver (MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag) were synthesized via a facile and inexpensive procedure and utilized to investigate photocatalytic degradation of tartrazine (TZN) in aqueous medium. The nanocomposites and their individual components were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), mapping, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS). The optimum conditions for TZN degradation obtained were TZN concentration of 20 mg/L and a solution pH of 6.5. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photoluminescence (PL) analysis were employed to evaluate the TZN degradation by the prepared MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposites. XRD of CQDs, MoS2 and Ag NPs showed amorphous, hexagonal phase and face-centered cubic structures, respectively. Band gaps of 3.3 and 1.9 eV for CQDs and MoS2, respectively as obtained from UV–Vis DRS. The enhanced photo-degradation of TZN was achieved due to the synergistic effects between the nanocomposite constituents. Complete degradation of TZN occurred within 30 min with a rate constant value of 0.172 min−1, wherein oxygen radicals played a significant role compared to other species during the process of degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic degradation
CQD
MoS2
Dendritic Ag
Tartrazine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TZN of the synthesized nanocomposites
The pH effect on TZN degradation
Effect of photocatalyst dosage on TZN degradation
Effect of concentrations of TZN on photocatalytic degradation
Performance of the nanocomposites using EIS

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present study, derived from postdoctoral research project by Hakimeh Teymourinia, was supported by Iran's National Elites Foundation grant Number 241/5610 and the University of Zanjan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (left) and EDS analysis (right) of the MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.
PL spectra of CQDs, MoS2/CQD, and MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.
XPS analysis of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.
Absorption spectra of 20 mg/L TZN in the presence of light and photocatalyst individually.
Absorption spectra of 20 mg/L TZN in the presence 0.3 g/L of (a) CQDs, (b) MoS2/CQD, (c) MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag, and (d) plot of percentage photocatalytic degradation of TZN as a function of time.

(a) Logarithmic plot of concentration changes at different times compared to initial concentration of TZN and (b) rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of TZN in the presence of 0.3 g/L CQDs, MoS2/CQD, and MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag.

(a) Effect of pH on 20 mg/L TZN degradation utilizing 0.6 g/L MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite and (b) rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of TZN in the presence of 0.3 g/L MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag in different pH media.
(a) Effect of photocatalyst concentration on 20 g/L TZN degradation and (b) rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of TZN in the presence of different concentrations of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag.

(a) Effect of initial TZN concentration in photocatalytic process and (b) rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of different concentrations of TZN using 0.3 g/L MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.
(a) Nyquist plots of GCE, CQD/GCEMoS2/CQD/GCE, and MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag/GCE in 1 mM K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 solution and (b) LSVs of GCE modified with nanomaterials in 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution.
(a) Effect of scavengers on TZN degradation and (b) reusability of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite in TZN degradation.
Schematic illustration of the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism for MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.
Rct values of electrodes obtained using 1 mM K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 solution.
Comparison of degradation data of this research with other sources.

head.ce:title: Flower-like nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots, MoS2, and dendritic Ag-based Z-scheme type photocatalysts for effective tartrazine degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites of carbon quantum dots, molybdenum disulfide, and dendritic silver (MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag) were synthesized via a facile and inexpensive procedure and utilized to investigate photocatalytic degradation of tartrazine (TZN) in aqueous medium. The nanocomposites and their individual components were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), mapping, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS). The optimum conditions for TZN degradation obtained were TZN concentration of 20 mg/L and a solution pH of 6.5. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photoluminescence (PL) analysis were employed to evaluate the TZN degradation by the prepared MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposites. XRD of CQDs, MoS2 and Ag NPs showed amorphous, hexagonal phase and face-centered cubic structures, respectively. Band gaps of 3.3 and 1.9 eV for CQDs and MoS2, respectively as obtained from UV–Vis DRS.

The enhanced photo-degradation of TZN was achieved due to the synergistic effects between the nanocomposite constituents. Complete degradation of TZN occurred within 30 min with a rate constant value of 0.172 min−1, wherein oxygen radicals played a significant role compared to other species during the process of degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The majority of organic dyes widely used in textile industries are persistent in the environment, endangering the health of living organisms in aquatic environments [1]. Tartrazine (TZN), a water-soluble azo dye, is widely used in foods and pharmaceuticals, but is known to cause asthma in people after its constant exposure [2]. Current biological treatment methods are ineffective in removing these pollutants from wastewater sources [3], but they can be degraded using biological treatment, chemical oxidation, physical adsorption, and membrane separations [4]. However, these techniques have the drawbacks of ineffectiveness and incomplete degradation causing secondary pollutants [5]. Alternatively, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) developed using photocatalytic materials are eco-friendly and effective in removing contaminants from polluted wastewater sources [6,7]. Such photocatalytic materials produce electron-hole pairs, creating free radicals, thereby reacting with dye molecules or organic pollutants in the presence of UV or visible light irradiation [8–10]. The possibility of electron excitation from valence band to the conduction band as well as the creation of the band gap allows the semiconductor to generate electron-hole pairs upon illumination. These holes can then form highly reactive hydroxyl radicals by absorbing H2O or hydroxyl groups from the reaction media [11].

In previous literature, innumerable types of photocatalysts have been used for the photocatalytic degradation of TZN [12]. One of the most effective approaches is the development of Z-scheme heterojunctions [13] that are capable of separating photogenerated carriers effectively to produce active species, absorb light broadly, and alter the redox ability. In this regard, we have prepared new types of Z-scheme photocatalysts based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), MoS2, and dendritic Ag and used these for photocatalytic degradation of TZN. Compared to other nano carbons, CQDs offer high promise because of their distinctive porous shapes, large accessible exterior surface area, strong electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and chemical stability [14,15]. The CQDs have also received a considerable interest as electrochemical sensors in view of their properties originating from both graphene and carbons [16].

The CQDs are known to exhibit chemical stability, water solubility, inertness, and electron transport properties with high speed due to quantum confinement [17,18]. Different synthetic methodologies have been developed to synthesize these CQDs, including microwave-assisted methods, laser ablation, pyrolysis, and hydrothermal [19]. Of these, hydrothermal method has been widely explored due to its advantages of high efficiency, low cost, and simplicity [20]. MoS2 is a transition metal dichalcogenide (TMDC) produced by exfoliating mono or nano layers from the bulk material [21], which can be prepared by sandwiching the two layers of chalcogen (sulfur) atoms between the layers of molybdenum atoms; this approach has been used in electrocatalysis and photocatalysis [22]. Due to excellent optical absorption properties and high charge carrier potency, MoS2 and MoS2-based nanomaterials received much interest [23]. Because of their small band gap (near 1.2 eV), 2D MoS2 nanomaterials can cause high absorption in the visible region [24] applicable for hydrogen generation or decomposition of organic contaminants from polluted water [25]. Similarly, silver nanoparticles have been investigated as possible metallic-based photocatalysts because of their effectiveness to absorb visible light [26]. The plasmonic silver nanoparticles have excellent electrocatalytic activity, stability, repeatability, and optical cross-sectional properties [27,28]. Due to their high specific surface area, Ag dendrites with multilayer branching architectures are the best nanostructures to provide more active sites at the ends of their branches or between the neighboring Ag branches [29]. This has prompted us to develop novel Z-scheme type photocatalyst nanocomposites consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), MoS2, and dendritic Ag, which were used to investigate photocatalytic degradation of TZN.

Chitosan, sodium molybdate, sodium hydroxide, ethanol, potassium chloride, and silver nitrate were all purchased from Merck and Sigma Aldrich. Double distilled water was used to prepare the required solutions. To prepare TZN stock solution with a concentration of 100 mg/L, a calculated quantity of TZN was dissolved in water. Lower concentrations of TZN were prepared from the stock solution. X'-Pert Pro Powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) set to Cu-K radiation, FE-SEM model TESCAN MIRA3, and FT-IR model PerkinElmer spectrum were used to characterize the synthesized nanomaterials. The μ-Autolab (III) device containing Nova software was employed for measuring electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS). The electrochemical cell included glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode.

A mercury-xenon lamp (350 W) with an intensity of 380.8 μw/cm2 at wavelength smaller than that of 380 nm was employed for visible light and photocurrent tests. For photocatalytic experiments, the catalyst with a concentration of 0.3 g/L was added to TZN with a concentration of 20 mg/L in a volume of 150 mL, while the lamp was located at a distance of 50 cm. Before being exposed to the radiation, the mixture was stirred for 30 min. After turning on the lamp, sampling was done in 5-min interval, and its absorption spectrum was recorded in the range of 300–600 nm after centrifugation and separating the catalyst.

A 0.3 g of corn powder was dissolved in 30 mL DI water and agitated for 30 min. The solution was then transferred to an autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The final product was water-soluble, which was separated by centrifugation. The obtained brown colored solution included CQDs [30].

At first, 0.2 g of CQDs was dissolved in water that was added to a solution containing sodium molybdate and thiourea. The resulting mixture was stirred for 1 h at room temperature, transferred to an autoclave at 200 °C and kept in that condition for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, the produced MoS2/CQD nanocomposite was collected after cooling and washing with ethanol.

A 0.2 g of the synthesized MoS2/CQD was dispersed in 0.2 M AgNO3 solution and silver dendrites were synthesized in different time intervals using the method of galvanic replacement [31] in the presence of MoS2/CQD. The optimal conditions for the preparation of silver dendrites were at temperature of 25 °C and time 10 s [31]. In this research, the effect of synthesis time on the structure of the resulting MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag composite was studied and Ag-dendrites were grown onto MoS2/CQD nanocomposite for 10 s.

Fig. 1 displays FT-IR spectra of the hybrid CQDs, dendritic Ag, flower-like MoS2, flower-like MoS2/CQD, and flower-like MoS2/CQD/ dendritic Ag composites. The distinctive peaks of graphitic carbon are observed in the FT-IR spectra of CQDs. Alkyl C-H stretching, C = C stretching, C-C stretching, and = C-H bending vibrations are responsible for the doublet at 2922 cm−1, medium band at 1633 cm−1, broad bands at 1084 cm−1, and 533 cm−1, respectively. The surface hydroxyl functional moieties and chemisorbed water molecules seem to be responsible for the wide peak centered at 3449 cm−1 [32]. FT-IR spectrum of silver dendrite showed absorption bands at 806, 1389, 1629, and 3389 cm−1. Peaks at 3490 cm−1 to 2850 cm−1 range are attributed to O-H stretching of alcohol molecules. Peaks at 1625 cm−1 originated from the C-N stretch vibrations of arenes, 1379 cm−1 that is related to primary amides of N-H (bond) and -C-N- stretching vibration of amines, and 806 cm−1 corresponds to O-H (H-bonded), which are generally the mild bending vibrations of alcohol. The presence of NO3 – in the residual solution significantly increased 1385 cm−1 [33]. Fig. 1 depicts the absorption bands at 460 cm−1, 893 cm−1, 1403 cm−1, and 1641 cm−1 related to MoS2 [29]. The Mo-S bond is responsible for the band that appeared at 460 cm−1, whereas S-S bond is responsible for the band at 893 cm−1. In addition, the peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 are the feature bands of MoS2 [28]. The peak at 3428 cm−1 is the distinctive band of O-H group [34,35]. In the FT-IR spectrum of MoS2/CQD, all the peaks of MoS2 and CQDs are present confirming the successful synthesis of the nanocomposite. FT-IR spectrum of flower-like MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite is similar to that of MoS2/CQD, which suggests that Ag dendrites may not change the chemical structure of MoS2/CQD nanocomposite.

Crystalline structure of the synthesized materials was assessed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), displayed in Fig. 4 . The XRD pattern of CQDs showed a platy band at 22°, which is originated from the (002) plane that matches with JCPDS Card No, 75-0444. In addition, there are four sharp peaks in the XRD pattern of dendritic Ag nanostructure, which matches with JCPDS Card No. 01-087-0718. The peaks at 2θ of 38.8°, 44.5°, 64.6°, and 77.6° can be attributed to (111), (200) and (220), and (311) crystallographic planes of the face-centered cubic (fcc) silver crystals, respectively [36]. For pure MoS2, diffraction peaks of the hexagonal phase, which are well matched with the JCPDS Card No. 37-1492, appear at 14.1°, 33.2°, 49.1°, and 59.3° corresponding to (002), (101), (103), and (110) planes, respectively [34]. In the XRD patterns of MoS2/CQD and MoS2/CQD/Ag nanocomposites, the peak positions and crystallinity of MoS2 are retained. The diffraction peaks of CQDs were not observed probably due to the amorphous structure of CQDs.

In the case of MoS2/CQD/Ag nanocomposite, the intensity of MoS2 peaks was weaker than that of the pure MoS2 indicating that continuous crystal growth has been repressed by the incorporation of CQDs on the flower-like MoS2 nanosheets. In addition, the presence of weak peaks of Ag at 38.8°, 44.5°, and 77.6° in the final nanocomposite indicates the presence of silver crystals in MoS2/CQD/Ag structure. Broadening of the peaks and decrease in their intensity indicate the formation of structures with a lower crystalline degree than pure Ag and MoS2 materials, which is because of the amorphous structure of CQDs [37].

Recombination of photo-generated charge carriers in the photocatalyst was studied using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and fluorescence intensity was used to determine the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. Higher fluorescence intensity indicated more recombination of charge carriers and vice versa. Fig. 5 shows PL spectrum of CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. As can be observed, the fluorescence emission spectrum for CQDs has a high intensity at the wavelengths of 450–500 nm. The fluorescence peak intensity was greatly reduced in the presence of MoS2 and dendritic Ag, indicating a very good charge transfer ability and excellent separation of the charge carriers of MoS2/CQDs/dendritic Ag nanocomposite. The PL spectra exhibited a redshift and broadened in the emission peak from 450 to 500 in the presence of Ag nanoparticles, which was likely due to the Z-scheme MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag heterojunction, resulting in an effective combination of CQDs, MoS2, and dendritic Ag [38]. This process can be attributed to synergistic photocatalytic effects of dendritic Ag and MoS2 loaded in the CQDs. The Ag nanoparticles respond to visible light by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect and hence, an increase in light harvesting and charge transitions. The excellent photocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite is due to the high surface area and good charge carrier’s separation that is originated from the synergistic effects of its constituents.

Fig. 6 shows the Tauc plot of (αhν)1/2 vs. (hν) data obtained from the UV–visible absorption spectra. The bandgap energy was obtained by extrapolating the straight line of the plot of (αhν)1/2 vs. (hν). Bandgaps of 3.3 and 1.9 eV were obtained for CQDs and MoS2, respectively. The calculated Eg of pure materials are in approximate agreement with the recent studies [39,40].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests were carried out to investigate the phase state and chemical composition of the as-synthesized MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposites. The survey spectra in Fig. 7 a clearly show the binding energy peaks from Ag 3p, Ag 3d, C 1s, C 2p, O 1s, Mo 3s, Mo 3d, Mo 3p, Mo 4s, and S 2p, indicating that the synthesized nanocomposite is composed of Ag, C, O, Mo, and S elements. The high-resolution Mo 3d XPS has two major peaks at 231.3 and 239.5 eV, respectively as shown in Fig. 7b, due to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, which correspond to Mo(IV) species [41]. In addition, the peak at 226.4 eV, which corresponds to Mo-S bond, is related to S 2s [42]. The observation of O 1s at 532 eV, Mo 3s at 500 eV, and two obvious peaks at 610 eV and 575 eV belong to Ag 3p1/2 and Ag 3p3/2 peaks in Fig. 7c, which suggest the presence of crystalline Ag. These results validate the substantial occurrence of Ag in its metallic state [43]. Three distinct aspects that represent three various chemical states in carbon may be extracted from the high-resolution C 1s peak of CQDs as shown in Fig. 7d. The graphitic structure of C = C is responsible for the peak at 285 eV, whereas C-C bonds are responsible for the peak at 285.8 eV [44]. In addition, C-O is associated with large peaks at 287.8 eV and the existence of C = O is inferred from the peak at 288 eV [45]. Thus, all the above-mentioned characterizations confirm the final synthesis of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TZN of the synthesized nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Performance of the synthesized nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation of 20 mg/L TZN was investigated at pH = 6.5 under the visible light. Fig. 8 depicts absorption spectra of 20 mg/L TZN only in the presence of light, where TZN showed an absorption peak with a maximum wavelength of 426 nm. The TZN degradation was less than 1% only by the light irradiation. Moreover, when MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite was added to TZN solution in dark condition, only 2% of TZN was degraded after 30 min, which is a very small amount. Therefore, it can be concluded that degradation of TZN cannot take place solely under the visible-light irradiation or in the presence of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite in dark condition.

The TZN degradation by the visible light irradiation in the presence of each of the synthesized nanocomposites reveals the photocatalytic performance of CQDs, MoS2, and dendritic Ag towards TZN degradation. Fig. 9 a shows absorption spectra of TZN in the presence of light and CQDs after 30 min. According to this figure, the intensity of absorption spectra of TZN decreased after 30 min, and the maximum degradation (%) of TZN in the presence of CQDs was equal to 39.36%. Moreover, TZN degradation (%) increased to reach 62.76% in the presence of MoS2/CQD nanocomposite. The highest TZN degradation (%) is due to MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite, which has almost completely degraded the TZN in 60 min. To study the photocatalytic properties further, the degradation rate constant of TZN in the presence of the synthesized nanocomposites was calculated using: (ln (
C i
C 0
) = -kt). The photocatalytic degradation rate constant of TZN in the presence of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite, CQDs, and MoS2/CQD were calculated to be 0.019, 0.035, and 0.172 min−1, respectively. The higher rate constant and better photocatalytic degradation (%) of TZN in the presence of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag compared to the other synthesized catalysts is due to larger surface area and more active sites of this nanocomposite, which has provided a better absorption capacity and therefore more efficient photocatalytic performance. BET analysis showed that the surface area of ternary nanocomposite is higher than the binary nanocomposite and CQDs. BET results revealed that MoS2 and dendritic Ag enhanced the CQDs surface area to create more active sites to incur photocatalytic degradation of TZN. The calculated results revealed that dendritic Ag, MoS2, CQDs, MoS2/CQD, and MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag have BET surface areas of 360.12, 80.31, 117.08, 148.20, and 570.02 m2/g, suggesting that activity of the photocatalyst was affected primarily by its surface area (see Fig. 10 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH effect on TZN degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect on degradation efficiency of 20 mg/L TZN with aid of 0.6 g/L MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag was investigated in the range of pH = 2.5–10.5, as shown in Fig. 11 . The obtained data showed the highest TZN degradation (%) in TZN solution of pH 6.5, which proceeded with the complete TZN degradation. The specific nanostructure of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag provided numerous active sites with proper interactions with TZN molecules, which was responsible for improved degradation of TZN. At this pH, TZN molecules with a negative charge were possibly better adsorbed by the nanocomposite with the positive surface charge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of photocatalyst dosage on TZN degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Different dosages of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag (0.15–0.5 g/L) were utilized to degrade 20 mg/L TZN, data shown in Fig. 12 a. The concentration of TZN did not change significantly in the absence of the catalyst, but by the addition of the catalyst, degradation rate of TZN initially increased, reached the maximum value at 0.3 g/L, and then declined with a further increment of the amount of photocatalyst. By increasing the quantity of the catalyst, light scattering and turbidity of the solution increased, and hence the number of photons contacting the TZN decreased, leading to decreased photocatalytic effect of the nanocomposite. Therefore, the optimal value of the catalyst concentration was considered 0.3 g/L. In this concentration, more active sites are available for TZN degradation, and as a result, catalytic process was quite effective. The plot of % degradation photocatalytic degradation rate constant in different doses of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag is displayed in Fig. 12b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentrations of TZN on photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation is strongly dependent on pollutant concentration. Hence, TZN solution with different concentrations (10–40 mg/L) was used to study the photocatalytic performance as shown in Fig. 13 a. The photocatalytic degradation of TZN changed from 100 to 62% for TZN concentrations from 10 to 40 mg/L, probably due to the decrement of hydroxide radicals/TZN concentration ratio; as a result, TZN % degradation decreased at higher concentrations. The effect of photocatalytic degradation rate constant in different concentrations of TZN in the presence 0.3 g/L MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag is shown in Fig. 13b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the nanocomposites using EIS
ce:section.ce:para:
EIS was used to investigate the charge transfer resistance of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with the synthesized nanomaterials. At first, GCE was well polished using the slurry of alumina powder and washed with water and acetone. Then, surface was activated by applying 10 consecutive cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution at the scan rate of 10 mV/s at the potential window of −1.0 to 1.0 V. Then, 1 mg of the synthesized nanomaterials was dispersed in DMF and 2 µL of the suspension was placed onto GCE surface. After drying, GCE surface at 50 °C, the EIS tests were performed in 1 mM K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.1 M potassium chloride. EIS results showed that charge transfer resistance of the GCE containing MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite is lower than the other nanomaterials, which is displayed in Fig. 14
[32,46,47].

Table 1 shows charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the GCE modified with the synthesized nanomaterials. The electrode modified with MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite showed the lowest Rct and hence, faster electron transfer rate confirmed the results of photocatalysis and PL experiments.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) method was also used to further investigate the performance of GCE modified with the synthesized nanomaterials. LSVs were recorded in a 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution under visible light in the potential range of −1.0 to 1.2 V at the scan rate of 50 mV/s (as shown in Fig. 14b), the nanocomposite showed the highest current density and as a result, better photocurrent response than the GCE modified with the other nanomaterials. Synergistic effect of the constituents of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite provided the best photocatalytic effect.

Photocatalytic degradation of 20 g/L TZN in the presence of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite was investigated during the 5 consecutive cycles, data displayed in Fig. 15 a. The results showed that photocatalytic degradation of TZN did not change much after 5 consecutive cycles, indicating the physical and chemical stability of the MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite. Table 2 compares photocatalytic degradation results of TZN of this work with the reported data. As per table data, photocatalytic degradation of TZN under the visible light reached to 100 % within 30 min, which is quite better than those reported in the literature.

Different scavengers were also used to investigate the contribution of species in photocatalytic degradation of 20 g/L TZN. Various scavengers such as EDTA-2Na as a hole scavenger (h+), benzoquinone (BQ) as an oxygen radical scavenger (●O2 –), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a hydroxyl radical scavenger (●OH) were used. Photocatalytic degradation of TZN was determined in the presence of each of these scavengers, as shown in Fig. 15a. Photocatalytic degradation of TZN in the presence of BQ, EDTA-2Na, and IPA were 35, 45, and 80%, respectively, suggesting that oxygen radical had the greatest contribution for the degradation of TZN in the presence of the prepared nanocomposite. The contribution of active species was in the order: ●O2 – > h+ > ●OH. In addition, reusability of the nanocomposite in TZN degradation showed that the developed nanocomposite could photocatalytically degrade 80% of TZN after five reuses. In fact, after five cycles, efficiency of the nanocomposite decreased by 0.7%, as can be seen in Fig. 15b.

To fully comprehend the mechanism of photochemical TZN degradation, it is necessary to recognize the type of the heterojunction produced between the photocatalyst components. Realizing the charge transfer mechanism and band locations in MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite is crucial. The proximal energy levels of the VB and CB locations of MoS2 and CQDs were estimated and the energy levels of MoS2 CB and VB were calculated using the equations below [55,56]:
(1) EVB = X – Ee + 0.5Eg
(2) ECB = EVB – Eg

Here, X denotes electronegativity of the catalyst material, Eg denotes the band gap energy, and Ee is the free electron energy (4.5 eV on the hydrogen scale). The electronegativity of MoS2 is predicted to be 5.32 eV [57]. Using the foregoing calculations, the CB potential for MoS2 was calculated to be −0.13 eV, while the VB potential to be 1.77 eV.

The electronic band gap was determined using the cyclic voltammetry and the difference in energy between HOMO and LUMO at zero temperature. The electrical band gap was 3.5 eV, which is quite near to the optical band gap (3.3 eV) of CQDs. The electronic band gap, HOMO, and LUMO levels of organic compounds were calculated using the following equations. The LUMO level was determined from the start [58,59].
(3) E LUMO = - E red − 4.75 (eV)
(4) E HOMO = - E ox − 4.75 (eV)
(5) E LUMO = E HOMO – E gap

Under the solar light radiation, CQDs and MoS2 may be readily stimulated to form e-/h+ couples. The photocatalytic test revealed that MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite had higher photocatalytic efficiency in the TZN degradation. The dendritic Ag is predicted to adopt the binding sites between MoS2 and CQDs that can create mutual surface and work as an electron transporter (Scheme 1 ). The following reactions were hypothesized for the role of photo-induced e-/h+. The dendritic Ag at the contact can operate as a bridge to enhance the electron transmission between CQDs and MoS2. Electrons can destroy TZN by producing the •O2 − active radicals, which increased the photocatalytic efficiency. By immobilizing the photo-excited dendritic Ag, it played an important role as an effective electron mediator [39]. All the photocatalyst components (CQDs and MoS2) were stimulated concurrently by the solar light, while the dendritic Ag can be (i) reduced to Ag*(e-) by migrating electrons in the CB of CQDs and MoS2 to Ag, and (ii) created the electron-hole cause of surfaceplasmonresonance (SPR). The Ag*(e-) was re-oxidized by the electrons passing through O2 molecules, resulting in •O2 − species [60]. As a result, the dendritic Ag acts as electron acceptor and mediator, while the electron migration improved the charge separation, delaying quick recombination of charge pairs onto CQDs and MoS2 surfaces. In photocatalysis, SPR of Ag may also boost the visible light absorption and photocatalytic activity by breaking the interfacial energy barrier via hot electron injection process from Ag to CB of CQDs and MoS2. Furthermore, owing to photoactivity and strong electron transferability, the dendritic Ag-connected sites between CQDs and MoS2 can operate as an additional driving force to incarease the charge transmission, thereby improving the charge separation [61].
(6) MoS2 + hv → MoS2 (e-) + MoS2 (h+)
(7) CQDs + hv → CQDs (e)- + CQDs (h+)
(8) CQDs (e-) + O2 → CQDs + •O2 −
(9) MoS2 (e-) + O2 → MoS2 + •O2 −
(10) CQDs (e-) + Ag → Ag*(e-) + CQD
(11) MoS2 (e-) + Ag → Ag *(e-) + MoS2
(12) Ag *(e-) + O2 → Ag + •O2 −
(13) CQDs (h+), MoS2 (h+) + H2O → •OH
(14)
•OH, •O2 − + TZN → Degradation products

Photocatalytic degradation of TZN was investigated by the developed MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposites under optimal conditions of 20 mg/L TZN, pH of 6.5, and time of 30 min. Very high specific surface area of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite provided many sites available for TZN degradation. On the other hand, a high electron transfer rate onto the surface of the catalyst was confirmed, which may be due to the very good separation of charges onto the CQDs surface in the presence of dendritic Ag and MoS2 nanoparticles and low charge transfer resistance of the MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite. In this study, a promising photocatalyst was developed for the first time and used for the degradation of TZN by benefiting from the synergistic effect of the components of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite. Additionally, the good physicochemical stability of the synthesized nanocomposites even during the five consecutive cycles suggested no change in the nanocomposite. Finally, the effect of different scavengers in the dye degradation showed that oxygen radicals have a great influence on the degradation of TZN. The simple and inexpensive synthesis method with 99.98% efficiency of TZN degradation in a relatively short time of 30 min can promise the prospect of wider scalability, production, and industrial-commercial application of MoS2/CQD/dendritic Ag nanocomposite.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present study, derived from postdoctoral research project by Hakimeh Teymourinia, was supported byIran's National Elites Foundation grant Number 241/5610 and the University of Zanjan.
